heading: SAGGIO INTRODUTTIVO LXXV
card_summary: Campanini's introduction continues its analysis of Zorzi's theology of Israel and the Church. The chunk covers four interlocking themes: Zorzi's christological reading of Isaiah's messianic prophecies (including the Hebrew title el gibbor, "Dio potente") against Jewish exegetes who applied them to Hezekiah; his typological principle that the Church as the "new Israel" must receive both the consolatory prophecies and the prophetic curses directed at the Jewish people; a remarkable and apparently novel parallel Zorzi draws between the sola fides controversy ignited by Luther and the ancient rabbinic-Karaite split within Judaism; and Zorzi's broadly sympathetic attitude toward living Jewish practice, illustrated by his use of the Sukkot (Feast of Tabernacles) celebration — including the etrog/citron — as exegetical evidence.
essay:
# Zorzi on Isaiah, the Church as Israel, and the Lutheran–Karaite Parallel

## The Christological Reading of Isaiah's Messianic Oracle

The chunk opens (pp. 75–76) with Zorzi's commentary on the famous Isaianic prophecy of the royal child (Isaiah 9). Campanini cites Zorzi reading the passage in rigorously christological terms, following unanimous Christian tradition, but the detail that receives special attention is the Hebrew title *el gibbor* ("Dio potente"). Zorzi dismisses Jewish interpreters who applied the prophecy to Hezekiah as *scioli Hebraeorum* — the less-prepared or half-learned among the Hebrews — charging them with culpable blindness:

> Anche Isaia afferma: Sulle sue spalle è il segno della sovranità e sarà chiamato: angelo del consiglio mirabile o 'consigliere ammirevole', Dio potente, padre del mondo a venire [...] perciò aggiunge che sarà chiamato principe della pace.

<cite>p. 75</cite>

Zorzi marshals the historian Eusebius and the Jewish historian Josephus to establish that neither the kingdom of Hezekiah nor the Davidic dynasty survived in any meaningful sense, leaving only the lineage of Christ, reduced to poverty and willingly excluded from royal splendor in anticipation of a different kingdom. The exegetical insistence that Jewish interpreters *delirant* — that their reading is sheer delirium devoid of logic — is noted by Campanini as characteristic of Zorzi's anti-Jewish polemic, even while Zorzi simultaneously draws heavily on Jewish sources.

## The Church as Verus Israel: A Bilateral Typology

Campanini identifies (pp. 76–77) one of the structural theological premises of the entire *De Harmonia Mundi*: the doctrine that the Church is the new and true Israel. The standard implication of this doctrine — that all consolatory Israelite prophecies are redirected to Christians — is familiar. What Campanini highlights as "una mossa interpretativa assai raffinata" (a notably refined interpretive move) is Zorzi's insistence on the bilateral logic of the principle:

> se la chiesa è l'autentico Israele, allora non solo le profezie consolanti ma anche tutte le maledizioni e le rampogne che la Bibbia, e in particolare i libri profetici, non risparmiano al popolo ebraico, vanno lette in riferimento ai cristiani, alla loro infedeltà, alla loro incredulità, al loro sostanziale, anche se non formale, rifiuto di Cristo e del suo vangelo.

<cite>p. 77</cite>

This inversion converts prophetic denunciations of Israel into mirrors of Christian failure, softening the triumphalist edge of the *verus Israel* topos by bending it reflexively back onto the Church. Campanini notes that while Zorzi does deploy the conventional image of the Synagogue as *prior sponsa* — the forsaken former bride — he simultaneously emphasizes the irrevocable nature of divine gifts and the extraordinary value of Israel's primordial election, especially in a cultural milieu that prized antiquity and chronological priority.

## The Law as *Lex Mortis* and the Pauline Framework

Zorzi develops (pp. 77–78) the Pauline antithesis between the written law as *lex mortis* and the law of Christ as the evangelical message of love. Campanini draws a revealing philosophical parallel: just as Zorzi the Platonist holds that the material world, though marked by corruptibility and the weight of matter, nonetheless finds its corresponding reality in the divine world and resonates harmoniously with it, so too the Old Testament — though marked by the harshness of sacrifice and the shadow of sin — does not merely receive its meaning from the Gospel but actively contributes to opening the way to Christ. The correspondence is described as "essenzialmente biunivoca" (essentially bidirectional): the Old Testament illuminates the New as much as the New fulfills the Old. This Platonic-typological hermeneutic is central to Zorzi's cosmological project: harmony operates at every level, including the exegetical.

## The Lutheran–Karaite Parallel: A Novel Comparison

The most striking passage in this chunk (pp. 78–79) is Zorzi's parallel between the *sola fides* controversy — already erupting in his day as Martin Luther's reform gathered force — and the medieval Jewish schism between Rabbanites and Karaites. Campanini identifies this parallel as, to his knowledge, unprecedented. Zorzi writes:

> Vi furono dunque non solo ai nostri giorni, ma in epoche assai remote e persino tra gli ebrei, taluni che vollero sostenere che la fede da sola è sufficiente alla salvezza: essi sono detti rabbini perchè si attenevano alle opinioni di alcuni maestri. Contro questi sorse un'altra setta, detta dei Carieni, perché seguivano soltanto la lettera del testo della sacra scrittura non accettando in alcun modo le opinioni degli uomini né le argomentazioni sofistiche dei maestri.

<cite>pp. 78–79</cite>

Campanini notes that Zorzi's mapping of the analogy is imprecise — the Reformation divide would crystallize not around *sola fides* versus *sola scriptura* but along different lines — and this imprecision is historically telling: the *De Harmonia Mundi* was composed when the theological ferment was still "magmatico," positions not yet crystallized. What matters for Campanini's argument is not the doctrinal accuracy but the gesture itself: Zorzi explicitly connects the internal fractures of Christianity with the internal fractures of Judaism, not polemically, but as evidence of "l'intima comunità di destino" (the intimate shared destiny) of the two religious traditions.

## Jacob's Blessing of Reuben and the Praise of Israel

Zorzi's commentary on Jacob's blessing of the twelve tribes (pp. 79) provides another example of his ambivalent but ultimately admiring stance toward Israel. Interpreting the blessing of Reuben typologically, Zorzi assigns the tribe to the zodiacal sign of Aries and its corresponding angelic power, and then breaks into what amounts to an encomium:

> Opportunamente il popolo giudaico [...] è detto 'figlio della visione' poiché quasi ogni visione e ogni profezia è stata concessa al popolo ebraico, il quale fu anche dotato del privilegio della primogenitura, secondo la testimonianza di Dio stesso che, nell'Esodo, dice: Israele è il mio primogenito [...] al giudeo prima, e poi al greco.

<cite>p. 79</cite>

The Pauline citation (Romans 1:16) is woven into Zorzi's praise of Jewish priority, reinforcing the sense that admiration for Israel's election is structurally necessary to his theology rather than incidental.

## Zorzi's Empirical Curiosity About Living Jewish Practice

Campanini closes this section (p. 79) with an observation about Zorzi's attitude toward contemporary Jewish practice. In his commentary on the four plant species of Sukkot (the Feast of Tabernacles), Zorzi displays what Campanini reads as direct observational knowledge of how the festival was actually celebrated by European Jews — including the substitution of the *etrog* (citron) with a lemon, a practice known to students of Ashkenazic and Italian Jewish custom. Campanini suggests one can almost picture Zorzi watching a real Sukkot celebration, whether in Padua, Venice, or the Holy Land, using living Jewish tradition as exegetical evidence to give the biblical text its full meaning. This empirical interest in Judaism as a living tradition, rather than merely a textual fossil, is presented as a key characteristic of Zorzi's scholarly personality and a foundation for his extensive use of kabbalistic sources.
